subject: History (Our Pasts - II)
chapter: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years

activities_projects.title: Cartographic Evolution Analysis
activities_projects.objective: Compare different historical perspectives of the Indian subcontinent
materials: Printouts of Al-Idrisi’s map (1154) and the French cartographer’s map (1720)
steps: Locate Sri Lanka on Al-Idrisi’s map and explain why it is placed at the 'top'
Identify the level of detail in the coastal areas of the 1720 map compared to the 1154 map
Explain how the change in information was linked to the needs of European merchant sailors
Discuss the term 'Cartography' and why a historian must be sensitive to the context of a map
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes how geographical knowledge and mapping techniques evolved over 600 years
activities_projects.evaluation: Ability to identify perspective shifts and the impact of information detail (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Historical Semantics Investigation
activities_projects.objective: Analyze how the meanings of political and social terms shift over time
steps: Research the use of 'Hindustan' by Minhaj-i-Siraj (13th century) vs. Babur (16th century)
List the geographical areas included in 'Hindustan' in both centuries
Contrast the medieval meaning of 'Foreigner' (pardesi/ajnabi) with the modern legal definition
Write a short paragraph on why using modern definitions for old texts can lead to historical errors
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that historical vocabulary is fluid and tied to specific political and social contexts
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct differentiation between territorial and cultural meanings of terms (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Scribe’s Manuscript Challenge
activities_projects.objective: Simulate the transmission of knowledge before the printing press
steps: Have five students copy a text sequentially (Student B copies from A, C from B, etc.)
Compare the final version with the original to find 'mutations' in words or sentences
Discuss how Ziyauddin Barani wrote his chronicle in 1356 and 1358 with significant differences
Explain why historians didn't know about Barani's first version until the 1960s
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates the reliability of manuscripts and the impact of manual errors on historical records
activities_projects.evaluation: Identification of errors and explanation of manuscript scarcity (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Technological and Crop Innovation Flowchart
activities_projects.objective: Trace the movement of ideas and goods across borders (700-1750 CE)
steps: Create a flowchart showing the arrival of the Persian Wheel, Spinning Wheel, and Fire-arms
Include crops like corn, potatoes, chilies, tea, and coffee
Explain the link between 'increased mobility of people' and the arrival of these innovations
Discuss how these new technologies changed the lives of common people (e.g., in irrigation or combat)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes the relationship between migration, technology, and economic change
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct categorization of innovations and their socio-economic impacts (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Jati Structure and Local Governance
activities_projects.objective: Explore the internal social hierarchies and rules of the medieval period
steps: Define 'Jati' and how status was determined (not fixed, but based on power/wealth)
Explain the role of 'Jati Panchayats' in managing the conduct of members
Investigate how Jati rules interacted with the overarching rules of the 'village' and the 'chieftain'
Discuss if traces of this system still exist in modern Indian villages
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the complexity of social organization and self-regulation in medieval India
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity in explaining the flexibility and hierarchy of the Jati system (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Rise of Patronage
activities_projects.objective: Examine the shifting power of Brahmanas and the emergence of new rulers
steps: Research why Brahmanas gained prestige through their knowledge of Sanskrit
Identify the term 'Patron' and explain how they supported Brahmanas to gain social status
Roleplay a meeting between a new 'Rajput' ruler and a Brahmana priest
Explain how this partnership helped consolidate the power of new dynasties
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the link between religious knowledge, social prestige, and political power
activities_projects.evaluation: Understanding of the symbiotic relationship between patrons and scholars (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Islam and the Indian Subcontinent
activities_projects.objective: Study the arrival and local adaptation of a major world religion
steps: Mark the trade routes through which the teachings of the Quran first reached India
Distinguish between the Shia (followers of Ali) and Sunni (followers of Khalifas) traditions
Explain how Islamic rulers (Ulma) and scholars interpreted the faith differently
Discuss the concept of Islam being a 'monotheistic' religion that appealed to many
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the diversity within Islamic traditions and their early history in India
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy in distinguishing Shia and Sunni traditions and the arrival of Islam (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Bhakti: A Social Revolution
activities_projects.objective: Understand the emergence of individual devotion
steps: Define 'Bhakti' as described in the chapter
Contrast Bhakti with 'elaborate temple rituals' led by high-caste priests
Analyze why Bhakti became popular among the lower social groups
Explain the idea of a 'personal deity' that can be reached without a mediator
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Explains the shift from ritual-based religion to emotion-based personal devotion
activities_projects.evaluation: Conceptual clarity on the Bhakti movement’s rejection of hierarchy (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Periodization: British vs. Modern Historians
activities_projects.objective: Critique how we divide history into blocks of time
steps: Examine James Mill’s division of history into Hindu, Muslim, and British periods
Identify why modern historians find this 'religious' division problematic
Explain the 'Medieval' period’s characteristics (spread of peasant societies, rise of states)
Argue why calling this period 'medieval' (in the middle) can sometimes be misleading
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Critically evaluates the labels used to categorize large spans of human history
activities_projects.evaluation: Strength of the argument against religious-based periodization (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Archive Management Project
activities_projects.objective: Learn the methodology of preserving historical evidence
steps: Define 'Archive' and list the types of records kept there (Official letters, accounts, land records)
Identify why paper became the dominant medium in the 13th-14th centuries
Create a mini-archive of family history (e.g., an old bill, a photo, a letter)
Write a short guide on 'How to protect old documents from moisture and pests'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the institutional importance of archives in maintaining historical continuity
activities_projects.evaluation: Quality of the mini-archive and understanding of archival value (10 marks)
